title: EA Operating Model
description: How the EA function organises, governs, and delivers architecture work — and what must change when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

components.label: EA Governance Model
components.description: How architecture decisions are made and enforced. Covers architecture boards, compliance, standards, and decision rights.

components.label: EA Organisation & Roles
components.description: Team structure, roles, competencies, and career paths for the EA function — including new roles for AI and agent governance.

components.label: EA Processes
components.description: How EA work gets done: the development cycle, reviews, and continuous architecture practices.

components.label: EA Repository & Tools
components.description: What tools and content support EA work — including new registries for models, agents, and machine-readable architecture.

components.label: EA Risk & Security
components.description: Risk management and security architecture for the agentic enterprise — including AI-specific threats and controls.

traditional.summary: Align business strategy with architecture direction and gain stakeholder buy-in.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Architecture Vision Development involves creating a high-level view of the desired architecture that aligns with business goals and stakeholder needs. It sets the direction for the architecture development process.

Tasks

  1. Stakeholder engagement: Identify key stakeholders and their concerns; conduct interviews to gather objectives, concerns, drivers and constraints.
  2. Business strategy alignment: Define the strategic goals and drivers and align the scope and constraints of the architecture effort.
  3. Develop the Architecture Vision including all relevant artefacts (e.g. business capabilities, application landscape).
  4. Communication to get approval and signoff.
  5. Baseline assessment (high-level) and roadmap definition, outlining the transition to the architecture vision.

Inputs

  • Business strategy and goals
  • Stakeholder concerns and requirements
  • Industry trends
  • Existing architecture documentation (e.g. architecture principles)

Outputs

  • Architecture Vision statement
  • High-level architecture models and roadmap
  • Stakeholder communication material
  • Statement of architecture work

ai_impact.summary: Must include <mark>AI operating model vision, agent-augmented process design intent, and AI value progression strategy</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: The Architecture Vision must evolve beyond traditional IT-business alignment to explicitly address how AI agents will participate in the enterprise. This means declaring which capabilities will be human-delivered, agent-augmented, or fully automated — and articulating the AI value progression from productivity gains to revenue generation. Stakeholder maps must expand to include AI platform teams, legal, HR, and workforce planning as first-class participants.
what_changes: Vision must declare agent deployment ambition: which capabilities will be human, augmented, or agent-native
Stakeholder maps must add AI platform teams, legal, HR, and workforce planning
Readiness assessment must include agent-specific factors: data quality, regulatory clearance, organisational willingness
what_survives: Misalignment between business and IT without a clear connection between the IT architecture effort and the business strategy
Lack of direction: teams are unsure what the future state looks like or why transformations are happening
Stakeholder resistance: key stakeholders are not engaged or do not support a transformation effort

traditional.summary: Provide a clear path towards a target architecture, aligned with business goals and priorities.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Architecture Roadmap Development involves creating a strategic plan that outlines the steps and milestones needed to achieve a target architecture. It provides a clear timeline and prioritization from the current state to the desired future state.

Tasks

  1. Current state assessment: Review and analyse the current state architecture to identify gaps, inefficiencies and opportunities.
  2. Target architecture definition: Reference and refine the target architecture (developed as part of the Architecture Vision).
  3. Gap analysis: Identify the gaps between the current state and the target architecture.
  4. Initiative identification: Define initiatives, projects or work packages required to address the gap.
  5. Roadmap development: Establish a timeline, including key phases and milestones.
  6. Resource planning: Identify the resource needs to execute the roadmap.

Inputs

  • Current state architecture
  • Target architecture
  • Business strategy and goals
  • Resource availabilities (budget, people, technology)

Outputs

  • Architecture Roadmap document
  • Transition architecture models
  • Implementation plans
  • Prioritized initiative backlog

ai_impact.summary: Roadmap horizons <mark>compress from years to months for AI components</mark>; must account for rapid model obsolescence.
ai_impact.body_html: AI technology evolves at a pace that renders traditional multi-year roadmaps obsolete before execution begins. Architecture roadmaps must adopt dual-speed planning: stable horizons for foundational infrastructure alongside compressed, iterative horizons for AI model selection, agent deployment, and capability evolution. Roadmaps must explicitly address model deprecation cycles and vendor diversification to avoid lock-in.
what_changes: Dual-speed roadmap horizons: stable for infrastructure, compressed for AI components
Must account for rapid model obsolescence and deprecation cycles
Roadmaps must include AI platform investment sequencing and vendor diversification
what_survives: Lack of direction and redundancies in the application landscape
Unrealistic and pressured timelines, due to a lack of strategic planning
Scope creep and too many scope changes

traditional.summary: Aligning with business goals and minimizing disruption through a structured and coordinated approach.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Transformation Planning involves developing a comprehensive plan to transition from the current state to the target architecture. It includes identifying the necessary steps, resources, and timelines to achieve the desired transformation.

Tasks

  1. Dependency analysis: Identify cross-project dependencies, risks and sequencing constraints.
  2. Work package definition: Break roadmap initiatives into actionable work packages with owners.
  3. Transition architecture design: Define intermediate states between current and target.
  4. Resource and budget allocation: Assign resources, budget and timelines per work package.
  5. Stakeholder alignment: Validate and approve the transformation plan with key stakeholders.

Inputs

  • Architecture Roadmap
  • Business strategy and goals
  • Resource availabilities (budget, people, technology)
  • Risk assessments and dependency information

Outputs

  • Transformation Plan document with work packages
  • Transition architecture models
  • Resource and budget allocation plan
  • Risk and dependency register

ai_impact.summary: Must <mark>sequence AI value progression (productivity to revenue), infrastructure investment, and process redesign</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: Transformation planning must account for the phased nature of AI value realisation. Organisations typically progress from productivity gains (phase one) to revenue generation (phase two), and transformation plans must sequence infrastructure investment, capability building, and process redesign accordingly. The interdependency between AI platform readiness and business transformation creates new sequencing constraints that traditional planning methods do not address.
what_changes: Must sequence AI value progression from productivity gains to revenue generation
Infrastructure investment for AI (GPU compute, vector stores, model serving) becomes a critical path dependency
Process redesign must be zero-based for agent strengths, not incremental automation of human workflows
what_survives: Rogue projects that are not in line with the overall strategy and enterprise architecture
Unrealistic timelines and project timelines that are not aligned with each other
Scope creep and unclear prioritization

traditional.summary: Ensure all needs are identified, understood and communicated throughout architecture development.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Architecture Requirements Management involves managing and governing the requirements throughout the architecture development process. It ensures that all requirements are identified, documented, and addressed in the architecture.

Tasks

  1. Identify and document requirements.
  2. Analyse requirements and prioritize based on business value, urgency and feasibility.
  3. Document requirements in a structured form (ensure traceability to business goals) and establish and monitor baseline requirements.
  4. Manage changes to requirements (use a repository to track changes and ensure version control).
  5. Traceability and impact analysis: Establish traceability between requirements and architecture decisions; assess the impact of changes.

Inputs

  • New stakeholder requirements
  • Business goals and objectives
  • Existing architecture documentation

Outputs

  • Architecture requirement specifications
  • Requirement impact assessment and prioritization
  • Updated requirements repository

ai_impact.summary: Must support <mark>continuous, machine-readable requirements</mark>; change velocity exceeds traditional governance capacity.
ai_impact.body_html: Requirements management must shift from periodic human intake processes to continuous, event-driven capture. AI components generate requirements at a pace that exceeds traditional governance capacity — model updates, agent behaviour changes, and infrastructure scaling needs emerge continuously. Requirements must become machine-readable to enable automated traceability, impact analysis, and compliance checking.
what_changes: Requirements must become machine-readable for automated traceability and compliance checking
Change velocity from AI components exceeds traditional governance capacity
Event-driven requirements capture replaces periodic manual intake
what_survives: Unclear requirements and misalignment, leading to stakeholder dissatisfaction
Scope creep where requirements keep getting added
Poor traceability between requirements, architecture and validation

traditional.summary: Facilitate effective decision-making by addressing specific stakeholder concerns.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Architecture View Creation involves developing various perspectives (views) of the architecture to address the concerns of different stakeholders. These views help in understanding, analysing, and communicating the architecture.

Tasks

  1. Identify information needs of EA stakeholders and their key questions and concerns.
  2. Select viewpoints and define which views address which stakeholder concerns (e.g. business capability map, application landscape, technology lifecycle).
  3. Develop architecture views using data from the architecture repository.
  4. Validate views with stakeholders and iterate based on feedback.
  5. Maintain and update views as architecture data evolves.

Inputs

  • Stakeholder requirements
  • Architecture Repository data
  • Viewpoint catalogue and standards (e.g. ArchiMate)
  • Architecture principles and guidelines

Outputs

  • Architecture views and models
  • Viewpoint catalogue
  • Validated and approved architecture views

ai_impact.summary: New views needed: <mark>agent interaction views, model portfolio views, AI infrastructure views, sovereignty compliance views</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: The viewpoint catalogue must expand significantly to address AI-specific stakeholder concerns. New views include agent interaction topologies, model portfolio dashboards, AI infrastructure capacity views, and sovereignty compliance maps. The pace of change in AI landscapes means views must shift from periodic manual creation to continuously updated, data-driven generation from the architecture repository.
what_changes: Viewpoint catalogue must add agent interaction, model portfolio, AI infrastructure, and sovereignty compliance views
Views shift from periodic manual creation to continuously updated, data-driven generation
New stakeholder groups (AI platform teams, ethics boards, regulators) require tailored perspectives
what_survives: Miscommunication between stakeholders and unclear views on the current situation
Overwhelming complexity of architecture views or lack of relevance
Creating views takes a long time with manual effort of multiple people

traditional.summary: Leverage new technologies to stay ahead of trends and achieve business goals.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

New Technology Evaluation involves assessing emerging technologies to determine their potential impact and value to the organization. It includes identifying, analysing, and recommending new technologies for adoption.

Tasks

  1. Technology scanning: Identify emerging technologies through market research, trend analysis and vendor engagement.
  2. Feasibility assessment: Evaluate technical fit, business value, risks and organizational readiness.
  3. Proof-of-concept: Conduct pilots or prototypes to validate assumptions.
  4. Recommendation: Create technology evaluation reports with clear adoption recommendations.
  5. Standards update: Feed approved technologies into the technology standards and roadmap.

Inputs

  • Business strategy and goals
  • Technology trends and market analysis
  • Existing technology landscape

Outputs

  • Technology evaluation reports and recommendations
  • Proof-of-concept and pilot results
  • Updated technology standards and approved technology list

ai_impact.summary: Must include <mark>model evaluation, agent framework assessment, and AI platform evaluation</mark>; commoditization of models changes selection criteria.
ai_impact.body_html: Technology evaluation must expand to cover an entirely new category: AI model and platform assessment. This includes benchmarking models for accuracy, cost, latency and sovereignty compliance; evaluating agent frameworks and orchestration platforms; and assessing AI infrastructure providers. The rapid commoditization of foundation models means selection criteria shift from raw capability to total cost of ownership, vendor lock-in risk, and sovereignty alignment.
what_changes: Model evaluation (benchmarking, cost/accuracy trade-offs, sovereignty compliance) becomes a core evaluation activity
Agent framework and orchestration platform assessment is a new evaluation category
Selection criteria shift from raw capability to total cost, lock-in risk, and sovereignty alignment
what_survives: Ad-hoc decision making has led to sub-optimal IT solutions
Overwhelmed teams and inability to decide on new technologies
New technologies have been implemented but are poorly integrated

traditional.summary: Identify and articulate the key factors that drive value creation for an organization.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Strategic Value Driver Definition is the process of identifying and articulating the key factors that drive value creation for an organization. These drivers are critical for aligning business strategies with operational activities to achieve long-term success.

Tasks

  1. Stakeholder engagement: Conduct interviews and workshops to gather insights on value creation.
  2. Value driver analysis: Analyse business processes and performance data to identify potential value drivers.
  3. Documentation: Define and document strategic value drivers, including their impact on business outcomes.
  4. Strategic alignment: Align value drivers with the organization's strategic goals and objectives.
  5. Communication: Communicate the identified value drivers to stakeholders through presentations and documentation.

Inputs

  • Business strategy and objectives
  • Performance data and analytics
  • Stakeholder insights and expectations
  • Industry benchmarks and best practices

Outputs

  • Strategic value driver documentation
  • Value driver impact analysis
  • Alignment matrix with business goals
  • Stakeholder communication materials

ai_impact.summary: AI value drivers differ: <mark>productivity gains, revenue from AI-augmented products, data monetization, agent efficiency</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: Traditional value drivers centred on cost reduction, revenue growth, and operational efficiency remain relevant but are insufficient. AI introduces entirely new value creation mechanisms: productivity gains from agent-augmented workflows, revenue from AI-powered products and services, data monetization opportunities, and operational efficiency from autonomous agent processes. Value driver frameworks must distinguish between phase-one productivity gains and phase-two revenue generation to align executive expectations.
what_changes: Value drivers must explicitly include AI-specific sources: productivity gains, AI-augmented products, data monetization
Must distinguish between phase-one (productivity) and phase-two (revenue) AI value progression
Measurement frameworks need AI-specific KPIs: model ROI, agent productivity multiplier, inference cost efficiency
what_survives: Value drivers are too vague or generic, making them impossible to measure or act upon
Disconnect between business strategy and EA in defining value drivers
No regular review as value drivers become outdated when business priorities shift

traditional.summary: Define what the organization does and how it delivers value.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Capability Definition involves identifying and defining the core capabilities that an organization needs to achieve its business objectives. It provides a structured approach to understanding what the organization does and how it delivers value.

Tasks

  1. Capability identification through workshops and interviews and leveraging industry reference architectures.
  2. Capability modelling: Define each capability in a structured way (e.g. capability map) and organize them into categories or levels (e.g. strategic, core, supporting).
  3. Capability documentation: Document each with a clear definition, description and key attributes.
  4. Capability mapping: Map each capability to architecture domains (processes, application, data, technology).
  5. Align with stakeholders and finalise.

Inputs

  • Business strategy, business objectives and operating model
  • Organizational structure and value chains
  • Industry reference models (e.g. BIAN, eTOM)
  • Existing capability documentation (if available)

Outputs

  • Business capability map (visual representation of business capabilities)
  • Capability descriptions (including maturity assessment)
  • Business capability mapping to architecture domains

ai_impact.summary: Must <mark>distinguish human-delivered, agent-augmented, and fully-automated capabilities</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: Business capability maps must evolve to classify each capability by its delivery model: human-only, agent-augmented, or fully autonomous. This three-way classification drives downstream architecture decisions about infrastructure, governance, and workforce planning. Capability maturity assessment must add an AI-readiness dimension that evaluates data quality, process codification, and regulatory clearance for agent deployment.
what_changes: Capability maps must classify delivery model: human-only, agent-augmented, or fully autonomous
Capability maturity assessment adds AI-readiness as a dimension
Industry reference models need extension to cover AI-native capabilities that have no traditional precedent
what_survives: Capabilities are defined too granularly or inconsistently, making the map unusable for strategic discussions
No business ownership of the capability map
Confusion between capabilities and processes leading to unclear scope

traditional.summary: Visualize and optimize business processes to align operations with strategic goals and drive efficiency.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Process Modelling involves creating visual representations of business processes to understand, analyse, and optimize how work is performed. It provides a clear view of activities, roles, systems, and information flows, enabling effective process design and improvement.

Tasks

  1. Identify, prioritize and document key business processes based on business impact, complexity, and transformation needs.
  2. Analyse and document detail processes including activities, decision points, inputs/outputs, roles and responsibilities using standard notation (e.g. BPMN).
  3. Design optimized future processes by identifying inefficiencies, bottlenecks, redundancies, and improvement opportunities.
  4. Validate alignment with business goals and technology. Refine process models with stakeholders.
  5. Maintain and update process models as business operations evolve.

Inputs

  • Business strategy and objectives
  • Stakeholder interviews and workshop results
  • Existing process documentation
  • System and application documentation
  • Performance metrics and pain points

Outputs

  • Current state (AS-IS) process documentation
  • Process improvement recommendations
  • Future state (TO-BE) process models with capability and technology mapping
  • Process detail documentation and descriptions

ai_impact.summary: Must model <mark>agent-native processes, not just human-process-plus-automation</mark>; zero-based process redesign for agent strengths.
ai_impact.body_html: Process modelling must move beyond adding automation steps to existing human workflows. Agent-native process design starts from agent strengths — parallel execution, continuous operation, pattern recognition — and designs processes from scratch. Process notation must distinguish human tasks, agent tasks, and human-agent handoffs. Escalation and exception handling become first-class process elements when agents handle the happy path.
what_changes: Zero-based process redesign starting from agent strengths rather than automating human workflows
Process notation must distinguish human tasks, agent tasks, and human-agent handoffs
Escalation and exception handling become first-class design elements when agents handle routine work
what_survives: Missing, inconsistent or outdated documentation of business processes
Lack of standardization in process modeling notation and tools
Disconnect between business processes and IT systems

traditional.summary: Confirm that an architecture is fit for purpose and identify any gaps or issues.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Architecture Validation involves ensuring that the developed architecture meets the defined requirements and aligns with business goals. It includes verifying the accuracy, completeness, and feasibility of the architecture.

Tasks

  1. Review architecture documentation against requirements, principles and standards.
  2. Perform compliance assessments against architecture policies and guidelines.
  3. Assess technical feasibility, risks and implementation readiness.
  4. Conduct validation sessions with key stakeholders to confirm fit for purpose.
  5. Document findings, gaps and recommendations for remediation.

Inputs

  • Architecture documentation
  • Requirements specifications
  • Business goals and objectives
  • Guiding principles, standards, policies and guidelines

Outputs

  • Validation reports
  • Compliance assessment results
  • Updated architecture documentation

ai_impact.summary: Must validate <mark>AI-specific requirements: model performance, agent behaviour, sovereignty compliance, cost constraints</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: Architecture validation must expand its criteria to cover AI-specific requirements that traditional validation frameworks do not address. Model performance benchmarks, agent behaviour predictability, sovereignty compliance for data flows to AI services, inference cost constraints, and bias monitoring requirements must all become standard validation checkpoints. Validation must also shift from periodic milestone reviews to continuous automated checking as AI component changes outpace traditional review cadences.
what_changes: Validation criteria must include model performance, agent behaviour, sovereignty compliance, and cost constraints
Shift from periodic milestone reviews to continuous automated validation
AI bias monitoring and fairness requirements become standard validation checkpoints
what_survives: Validation happens too late and issues are discovered during implementation when changes are expensive
No clear validation criteria making reviews subjective and inconsistent
Validation is seen as a bottleneck and projects try to skip or rush through it

traditional.summary: Design and document application structures to guide development and transformation.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Application Architecture Modelling involves designing and documenting the structure, components, and interactions of software applications. It provides a blueprint for how applications are composed, relate to each other, and support business capabilities.

Tasks

  1. Analyse the current application landscape — document applications, technology stack and lifecycle status.
  2. Define application architecture principles, standards and design patterns (e.g. microservices, layered, event-driven).
  3. Design target application architectures — model components, responsibilities, interactions and data flows.
  4. Identify integration points and dependencies with other architecture domains.
  5. Validate application models with stakeholders and iterate based on feedback.
  6. Maintain and update application models as requirements and the technology landscape evolve.

Inputs

  • Business strategy and objectives
  • Current state application documentation
  • Stakeholder requirements and expectations
  • Architecture Vision and principles
  • Application portfolio inventory

Outputs

  • Application architecture models (e.g. component diagrams, data flow diagrams)
  • Application architecture principles and standards
  • Integration points and interfaces documentation
  • Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)

ai_impact.summary: AI agents are <mark>first-class application components; model abstraction layers, agent orchestration, and multi-agent topologies</mark> are new patterns.
ai_impact.body_html: Application architecture must treat AI agents as first-class application components, not bolt-on integrations. This introduces entirely new architectural patterns: model abstraction layers that decouple applications from specific AI models, agent orchestration frameworks that manage multi-agent workflows, and topology patterns for agent-to-agent communication. The application portfolio must track AI models and agents alongside traditional applications, with lifecycle management for model versions, fine-tuned variants, and agent configurations.
what_changes: AI agents become first-class application components with their own lifecycle management
New patterns: model abstraction layers, agent orchestration, multi-agent topologies
Application portfolio must track AI models and agents alongside traditional applications
what_survives: No standardized way of documenting application architectures across teams
Architecture only done at project level with no enterprise-wide view
Architecture decisions are not documented, leading to repeated debates

traditional.summary: Integrating all applications and components of an IT landscape in an efficient and future-proof way.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Integration Architecture Modelling is the process of designing and documenting the interactions and data flows between different systems and applications within an enterprise. It ensures seamless communication and data exchange, enhancing overall system efficiency.

Tasks

  1. Identification of integration points and patterns between applications.
  2. Define integration architecture principles and standards.
  3. Develop detailed integration models, including data flow diagrams, interface specifications and communication protocols.
  4. Validate and verify integration models with stakeholders.
  5. Maintain and update integration models to reflect changes in the architecture.

Inputs

  • Architecture Vision and other architecture documentation (e.g. principles)
  • Stakeholder requirements and expectations
  • Integration points and interfaces documentation
  • Industry standards and best practices

Outputs

  • Integration architecture models, diagrams and reports
  • Integration architecture principles, integration patterns, tools and techniques

ai_impact.summary: <mark>Agent-to-agent communication, protocol abstraction (MCP/A2A), and semantic interoperability</mark> via knowledge graphs are new integration concerns.
ai_impact.body_html: Integration architecture faces a paradigm shift with the emergence of agent-to-agent communication protocols. MCP (Model Context Protocol), A2A (Agent-to-Agent), and ACP (Agent Communication Protocol) introduce new integration patterns that operate alongside traditional APIs and messaging. Integration architecture must define protocol abstraction layers that allow agent communication independent of specific protocol choice, and must address semantic interoperability through shared knowledge graphs and ontologies.
what_changes: Agent-to-agent communication protocols (MCP, A2A, ACP) create new integration patterns
Protocol abstraction layers needed to avoid lock-in to specific agent communication standards
Semantic interoperability via knowledge graphs replaces rigid schema-based integration for agent contexts
what_survives: Inadequate documentation of interfaces leads to unplanned efforts and delays
Overly complex IT landscapes due to lack of standardization
Duplication of efforts and missed opportunities due to missing overview of integration patterns

traditional.summary: Define and structure the key business entities and their relationships.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Business Object Modelling involves identifying, defining, and documenting the core business entities (objects). It establishes a common vocabulary and understanding of what data represents, including attributes, relationships, and business rules, as the base for the data architecture.

Tasks

  1. Identify core business objects through stakeholder workshops, process analysis, and business capability mapping.
  2. Define business objects, including their purpose, key attributes, definitions, ownership.
  3. Document business rules that govern business objects (e.g. validation rules, lifecycle states, constraints).
  4. Validate and align business objects with stakeholders and business processes.
  5. Maintain and evolve the business object model as the business changes.

Inputs

  • Business strategy and objectives
  • Business capability model
  • Process models and documentation
  • Existing data models and definitions
  • Stakeholder requirements

Outputs

  • Conceptual data model (business object model)
  • Business object definitions and glossary
  • Business rules documentation
  • Business object to process/capability mapping

ai_impact.summary: Must accommodate <mark>AI entities: models, agents, prompts, training datasets as business objects</mark> with lifecycle, ownership, and governance.
ai_impact.body_html: The business object model must expand to include AI-native entities that have no traditional precedent. AI models, agents, prompts, training datasets, embeddings, and evaluation benchmarks are all business objects that require lifecycle management, ownership assignment, and governance rules. These new entities have unique characteristics — models have versions, fine-tuned variants, and performance metrics; agents have behaviour specifications, permission scopes, and audit requirements.
what_changes: AI models, agents, prompts, and training datasets become first-class business objects
New lifecycle patterns: model versioning, fine-tuning lineage, prompt evolution tracking
Ownership and governance rules must address non-deterministic, evolving AI entities
what_survives: Inconsistent terminology across different departments and systems
Lack of clear data ownership resulting in poor data quality
Gap between business concepts and technical implementation

traditional.summary: Design logical and physical data structures that implement business objects.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Data Architecture Modelling involves creating detailed logical and physical data models that translate business objects into implementable data structures. It defines how data is organized, stored, and accessed, ensuring data consistency, integrity, and optimal performance.

Tasks

  1. Translate business objects into logical data models, defining entities, attributes, primary/foreign keys, and relationships.
  2. Define data types, constraints, and validation rules at the technical level.
  3. Apply data modeling principles including normalization, referential integrity, and data standardization.
  4. Create physical data models optimized for specific database platforms and performance requirements.
  5. Validate data models with stakeholders (data owners, architects, developers) and ensure alignment with business requirements.
  6. Maintain and evolve data models as business and technical requirements change.

Inputs

  • Conceptual data model (business object model)
  • Business requirements and use cases
  • Existing database schemas and data models
  • Performance and scalability requirements
  • Technology standards and database platforms

Outputs

  • Logical data models (entity-relationship diagrams)
  • Physical data models (database schemas and specs)
  • Data dictionary and metadata documentation
  • Data model to business object mapping
  • Data integrity and validation rules

ai_impact.summary: New data structures: <mark>vector stores, knowledge graphs, embeddings, training data catalogues</mark>. Sovereignty constraints on data flows to AI models.
ai_impact.body_html: Data architecture must expand to encompass entirely new data storage paradigms required by AI workloads. Vector databases for embedding storage and similarity search, knowledge graphs for semantic context, training data catalogues with provenance tracking, and feature stores for ML pipelines all become core data architecture components. Sovereignty constraints add a new dimension: data flows to AI models must respect jurisdictional requirements on data residency, model training data provenance, and cross-border inference.
what_changes: Vector stores, knowledge graphs, and embedding databases become core data architecture components
Training data catalogues with provenance tracking are required for AI governance
Sovereignty constraints on data flows to AI models become a first-order design input
what_survives: Inconsistent data models across systems, data silos and integration challenges
Lack of standardization in naming conventions, data types, and methods
Disconnect between logical and physical models creating implementation gaps

traditional.summary: Map and optimize how data moves between systems, applications, and processes across the enterprise.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Data Flow Modelling involves documenting and analyzing how data moves, transforms, and integrates across systems, applications, and business processes. It captures data sources, destinations, transformation logic, integration points, and dependencies.

Tasks

  1. Identify data sources and destinations across systems, applications, and external partners.
  2. Document data flows and transformations between systems.
  3. Define integration points and patterns (e.g. real-time, batch, API, ETL/ELT, messaging).
  4. Establish data lineage showing origin, movement, and transformation of data throughout lifecycle.
  5. Identify dependencies and impacts when a data source or flow is changed or unavailable.
  6. Validate data flows with stakeholders (data owners, integration architects, application teams).
  7. Maintain and update data flow models as integrations evolve.

Inputs

  • Business processes and requirements
  • Application architecture and integration landscape
  • Data architecture models (logical/physical)
  • Existing interface, API and integration documentation
  • Data quality and governance requirements

Outputs

  • Data flow diagrams (context, detailed level)
  • Data lineage documentation
  • Data transformation, dependency and impact analysis
  • Interface specifications

ai_impact.summary: New data flows: <mark>inference pipelines, training data pipelines, agent context data, RAG retrieval flows</mark>. Sovereignty must be visible.
ai_impact.body_html: Data flow models must capture entirely new categories of data movement that AI workloads introduce. Inference pipelines, training data preparation flows, RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) retrieval paths, agent context data flows, and embedding generation pipelines all create new data movement patterns. Critically, sovereignty constraints must be visible in data flow models — showing where data crosses jurisdictional boundaries on its way to AI processing services.
what_changes: New flow categories: inference pipelines, training data pipelines, RAG retrieval, agent context flows
Sovereignty constraints must be visible in data flow diagrams showing jurisdictional boundary crossings
Data lineage must track provenance through AI processing stages including model training and fine-tuning
what_survives: Undocumented or poorly documented integrations
Lack of data lineage visibility preventing impact analysis
Point-to-point integration spaghetti without governance

traditional.summary: Define and design the technical systems and infrastructure components.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

System Architecture Modelling involves designing and documenting the technical infrastructure and system components that host and run business applications. It defines servers, compute resources, operating systems, middleware, databases, and their configurations.

Tasks

  1. Identify system requirements from application architecture, business requirements, and non-functional requirements.
  2. Define system components, topology and configuration for servers, compute, operating systems, middleware, data platforms, and storage.
  3. Apply system architecture patterns and standards (e.g. high availability, load balancing, clustering, redundancy).
  4. Define sizing and capacity requirements based on workload analysis and growth projections.
  5. Document security and compliance and validate system architecture with stakeholders.
  6. Maintain and evolve system architecture as technology and business requirements change.

Inputs

  • Application architecture and requirements
  • Non-functional requirements
  • Technology standards and reference architectures
  • Existing infrastructure, capacity and performance data
  • Budget and cost constraints

Outputs

  • System architecture diagrams and models
  • System component, capacity and sizing specifications
  • Deployment architecture documentation
  • High availability and disaster recovery designs
  • Security and compliance requirements

ai_impact.summary: Must cover <mark>GPU compute, model serving platforms, vector databases, agent runtime environments</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: System architecture must expand to cover an entirely new infrastructure tier for AI workloads. GPU compute clusters for training and inference, model serving platforms with auto-scaling, vector databases for embedding storage, agent runtime environments with orchestration, and specialised storage for training data and model artifacts all become core system architecture components. Capacity planning must account for the bursty, GPU-intensive nature of AI workloads and the rapid scaling requirements of agent deployment.
what_changes: GPU compute clusters, model serving platforms, and vector databases become core infrastructure components
Agent runtime environments with orchestration and scaling capabilities are new platform requirements
Capacity planning must account for bursty, GPU-intensive AI workloads
what_survives: Inconsistent system designs across different applications and environments
Over- or under-provisioning due to lack of proper sizing and capacity planning
High operational costs from inefficient infrastructure design and complexity

traditional.summary: Design and optimize the network infrastructure that enables secure and reliable connectivity.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Network Architecture Modelling involves designing and documenting the network infrastructure and connectivity that enables communication between systems, applications, users, and external partners. It defines the network topology and its components.

Tasks

  1. Identify network requirements from application architecture, business requirements, and non-functional requirements.
  2. Design network topology, segmentation, security zones based on security and compliance requirements.
  3. Define connectivity, capacity and performance for different scenarios (site-to-site, remote access, cloud connectivity, partner integration).
  4. Apply network architecture patterns and standards (e.g. redundancy, high availability, traffic management, Quality of Service).
  5. Validate network architecture with stakeholders. Document security and compliance requirements.
  6. Maintain and evolve network architecture as technology and business requirements change.

Inputs

  • Application architecture and connectivity requirements
  • Non-functional requirements
  • Technology standards and reference architectures
  • Existing network infrastructure, topology and usage data
  • Security and compliance policies, cost constraints

Outputs

  • Network architecture diagrams and topology models
  • Network component specifications and configurations
  • Network segmentation and security zone design
  • High availability, capacity and performance design
  • Security and compliance requirements

ai_impact.summary: AI workloads have <mark>distinct networking requirements: high-bandwidth GPU interconnects, inference latency optimisation</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: AI workloads introduce networking requirements that differ fundamentally from traditional enterprise applications. GPU cluster interconnects require ultra-high bandwidth and low latency for distributed training. Inference serving demands optimised network paths to minimise response latency. Agent-to-agent communication creates new traffic patterns with different QoS requirements. Sovereignty constraints may require network-level controls to ensure AI data flows remain within jurisdictional boundaries.
what_changes: High-bandwidth, low-latency GPU interconnects for distributed AI training become a design requirement
Inference latency optimisation drives network topology decisions for AI serving infrastructure
Agent-to-agent communication creates new traffic patterns requiring distinct QoS profiles
what_survives: Inconsistent network designs across environments creating complexity
Performance bottlenecks due to inadequate capacity planning or poor traffic management
Poor visibility into network traffic and security events

traditional.summary: Design virtualized and containerized infrastructure to optimize resource utilization, agility, scalability.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Virtualization Architecture Modelling involves designing and documenting virtualized compute, storage, and network resources, including virtual machines, containers, and cloud-based infrastructure.

Tasks

  1. Identify virtualization requirements from application architecture, business requirements, and non-functional requirements.
  2. Define virtualization strategy and platforms including hypervisors, container platforms, and cloud services.
  3. Define topology, orchestration, automation, scaling, and lifecycle management of virtualized resources.
  4. Apply virtualization patterns and standards (e.g. resource optimization, high availability, disaster recovery). Design for cloud-native and hybrid architectures.
  5. Document security and compliance requirements. Validate virtualization architecture with stakeholders.
  6. Maintain and evolve virtualization architecture as technology and business requirements change.

Inputs

  • Application architecture and requirements
  • Non-functional requirements (performance, scalability, availability, portability)
  • Technology standards and reference architectures
  • Existing virtualization and cloud infrastructure

Outputs

  • Virtualization architecture diagrams and models
  • Platform specifications, container and orchestration designs
  • Resource allocation and capacity specifications
  • Cloud integration and hybrid architecture designs

ai_impact.summary: Must cover <mark>GPU virtualisation, container orchestration for AI workloads, serverless inference, multi-cloud AI deployment</mark> for sovereignty.
ai_impact.body_html: Virtualization architecture must extend to address the unique compute requirements of AI workloads. GPU virtualisation and sharing (e.g. MIG, time-slicing) enable efficient utilisation of expensive GPU resources. Container orchestration must support AI-specific scheduling for GPU affinity, memory requirements, and batch training jobs. Serverless inference platforms offer cost-efficient scaling for variable AI workloads. Multi-cloud deployment becomes a sovereignty requirement when AI processing must stay within specific jurisdictions.
what_changes: GPU virtualisation and sharing (MIG, time-slicing) becomes a core design concern
Container orchestration must support AI-specific scheduling: GPU affinity, memory requirements, batch training
Multi-cloud AI deployment becomes a sovereignty requirement for jurisdictional compliance
what_survives: Inconsistent virtualization approaches across environments creating complexity
Resource sprawl and inefficient utilization due to lack of governance and monitoring
Vendor lock-in from over-reliance on proprietary virtualization or cloud platforms

traditional.summary: Turn architecture data into actionable insights for informed decision-making.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Designing, building and maintaining visual dashboards, standardized views and reports that present architecture data in a clear, actionable format for different stakeholder groups.

Tasks

  1. Identify reporting needs and key questions per stakeholder group (e.g. CIO, project managers, domain architects).
  2. Define and design dashboard layouts, views and report templates.
  3. Implement dashboards and reports using the EA tooling platform (e.g. LeanIX, Mega, ServiceNow).
  4. Automate data feeds from the architecture repository to dashboards where possible.
  5. Maintain, update and continuously improve dashboards and reports based on stakeholder feedback.

Inputs

  • Architecture Repository data (applications, technologies, capabilities, etc.)
  • Stakeholder reporting requirements
  • EA governance KPIs and metrics
  • Data quality assessments

Outputs

  • Stakeholder-specific dashboards and views
  • Standardized architecture reports (e.g. application landscape, technology lifecycle, capability coverage)
  • KPI and metric visualizations

ai_impact.summary: New dashboards: <mark>AI FinOps, agent performance, model portfolio, sovereignty compliance, automated compliance status</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: The dashboard and reporting portfolio must expand significantly to cover AI-specific operational concerns. AI FinOps dashboards track inference costs, model utilisation, and budget consumption. Agent performance dashboards monitor task completion, error rates, and escalation patterns. Model portfolio views show model versions, benchmarks, and deprecation timelines. Sovereignty compliance dashboards map data flows against jurisdictional requirements. These new dashboards require real-time data feeds from AI platforms, not periodic manual updates.
what_changes: New dashboard categories: AI FinOps, agent performance, model portfolio, sovereignty compliance
Dashboards require real-time data feeds from AI platforms and agent runtime environments
Automated compliance status reporting replaces periodic manual compliance assessments
what_survives: Reports are created manually and ad-hoc, leading to inconsistency and high effort
Dashboards exist but are not used because they don't answer the right questions
Data in reports is outdated or inaccurate due to poor data quality in the repository

traditional.summary: Ensure architecture decisions are based on reliable, complete and up-to-date data.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Establishing and operating processes to systematically collect, validate and maintain architecture data across the organization, ensuring a high level of data quality, completeness and consistency in the architecture repository.

Tasks

  1. Define the data scope and data model for the architecture repository (what entities, attributes and relationships are needed).
  2. Identify and connect data sources (e.g. CMDB, ITSM, HR systems, project portfolio tools).
  3. Establish data collection processes — automated feeds where possible, structured manual input where necessary.
  4. Define data quality rules, validation checks and quality KPIs (e.g. completeness, accuracy, timeliness).
  5. Assign data owners and stewards responsible for maintaining data in their domain.
  6. Conduct regular data quality reviews and remediation cycles.

Inputs

  • EA data model and repository structure
  • Existing data sources (CMDB, ITSM, PPM, HR, etc.)
  • Data quality requirements from stakeholders
  • Architecture governance policies

Outputs

  • Populated and up-to-date architecture repository
  • Data quality reports and scorecards
  • Data ownership matrix
  • Documented data collection processes and integration interfaces

ai_impact.summary: Must support <mark>automated data gathering from deployment pipelines, API gateways, and cloud inventory</mark>; machine-readable architecture data.
ai_impact.body_html: Architecture data gathering must shift from manual collection and periodic updates to automated, continuous feeds from live infrastructure. Deployment pipelines, API gateways, cloud management platforms, and AI model registries become primary data sources. Architecture data must become machine-readable so that AI agents can consume and act on it — enabling automated compliance checking, drift detection, and architecture-aware agent decisions.
what_changes: Automated data gathering from deployment pipelines, API gateways, and cloud inventory replaces manual collection
Architecture data must become machine-readable for AI agent consumption
New data sources: AI model registries, agent runtime environments, inference cost APIs
what_survives: Repository data is outdated, incomplete or inconsistent because no one owns it
Data is gathered manually via spreadsheets, leading to high effort and errors
No clear definition of what data is needed, resulting in too much noise or critical gaps

traditional.summary: Keep the architecture repository operational, reliable and fit for evolving organizational needs.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Managing the day-to-day operations, technical maintenance and continuous improvement of the architecture repository platform, ensuring its availability, usability and alignment with evolving EA requirements.

Tasks

  1. Manage the EA repository platform (e.g. LeanIX, Mega, ServiceNow) including configuration, access management and upgrades.
  2. Maintain and evolve the repository meta-model and data structure as EA requirements change.
  3. Manage integrations with connected systems (CMDB, ITSM, PPM, etc.) and ensure data flows are operational.
  4. Provide user support — handle requests, issues and questions from architects and stakeholders.
  5. Develop and maintain user guidelines, templates and onboarding materials.
  6. Monitor platform usage, performance and adoption metrics; identify and implement improvements.

Inputs

  • EA repository platform and infrastructure
  • User feedback and support requests
  • EA meta-model and data model requirements
  • Integration specifications and SLAs with connected systems

Outputs

  • Operational and up-to-date repository platform
  • Updated meta-model and repository configuration
  • User guidelines, templates and training materials
  • Platform usage and adoption reports, resolved support tickets

ai_impact.summary: Repository must accommodate <mark>new registries (agent, model), machine-readable content, and continuous automated updates</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: The architecture repository must evolve from a human-curated documentation system to a machine-readable, continuously updated platform that supports both human architects and AI agents. This requires new registries for AI models (versions, benchmarks, cost profiles) and agents (configurations, permissions, performance metrics), machine-readable content formats that agents can query and update, and integration with AI platform APIs for continuous automated updates. The meta-model must expand to accommodate AI-native entities and relationships.
what_changes: New registries: AI model registry (versions, benchmarks, costs) and agent registry (configurations, permissions, metrics)
Content must be machine-readable for AI agent consumption and automated updates
Meta-model must expand to accommodate AI-native entities and relationships
what_survives: The repository tool is set up but nobody maintains it and integrations break
Low adoption because users find the tool complex, unclear or poorly documented
No dedicated ownership so repository maintenance is everyone's and no one's job

traditional.summary: Build and evolve the EA organization to effectively deliver architecture capabilities.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Designing, establishing and continuously improving the EA organizational structure, including team setup, roles, competencies and career paths, to ensure the EA function is adequately staffed and positioned within the organization.

Tasks

  1. Define the EA organizational model and reporting structure (e.g. centralized, federated, hybrid).
  2. Define EA roles, job profiles and required competencies per role.
  3. Assess current team capabilities against required competencies — identify gaps.
  4. Develop hiring plans, training paths and career development frameworks for EA staff.
  5. Continuously evaluate and adapt the EA organization to changing business needs and maturity levels.
  6. Foster collaboration models between central EA and domain/solution architects across the organization.

Inputs

  • Business and IT strategy
  • Current EA team structure and skill profiles
  • EA capability maturity assessment results
  • HR policies and frameworks

Outputs

  • EA organizational model and reporting structure
  • EA role descriptions and competency matrix
  • Skills gap analysis and development plans
  • Collaboration model between central and federated EA roles

ai_impact.summary: New roles: <mark>AI/Agent Architect, Model Portfolio Manager, AI Ethics Officer, Agent Ops Manager</mark>. AI literacy as baseline for all EA roles.
ai_impact.body_html: The EA organisation must evolve to include entirely new roles that address AI-specific architecture concerns. AI/Agent Architects design agent topologies and orchestration patterns. Model Portfolio Managers govern the enterprise's AI model selection and lifecycle. AI Ethics Officers ensure responsible AI deployment aligned with organisational values and regulations. Agent Ops Managers handle the operational management of deployed agents. Beyond new roles, AI literacy becomes a baseline competency for all EA roles — every architect must understand AI capabilities, limitations, and governance requirements.
what_changes: New roles: AI/Agent Architect, Model Portfolio Manager, AI Ethics Officer, Agent Ops Manager
AI literacy becomes a baseline competency requirement for all EA roles
Organisational model must accommodate human-agent collaboration patterns in EA delivery
what_survives: EA roles are unclear or overlapping, leading to confusion about who does what
EA team is understaffed or lacks critical skills, but no structured plan exists to address this
EA is positioned too far from business or too deep in IT, limiting its effectiveness

traditional.summary: Measure, evaluate and demonstrate the value and effectiveness of Enterprise Architecture.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Establishing and operating a framework to define, measure and report on the performance and value contribution of the EA function, ensuring continuous alignment with business objectives and stakeholder expectations.

Tasks

  1. Define EA performance goals aligned with business and IT strategy.
  2. Identify and establish KPIs and metrics (e.g. stakeholder satisfaction, architecture compliance rate, time-to-decision, repository adoption).
  3. Implement measurement and data collection mechanisms.
  4. Produce regular performance reports and present to key stakeholders.
  5. Analyse results, identify improvement areas and drive corrective actions.

Inputs

  • Business and IT strategy and goals
  • EA capability maturity assessment results
  • Stakeholder feedback and satisfaction data
  • Repository usage and data quality metrics

Outputs

  • EA KPI framework and metric definitions
  • Periodic EA performance reports and dashboards
  • Improvement recommendations and action plans
  • Stakeholder communication on EA value contribution

ai_impact.summary: Must include <mark>AI-specific value metrics: model ROI, agent productivity gains, AI FinOps efficiency</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: EA performance management must expand its KPI framework to capture the value contribution of AI-related architecture decisions. Model ROI metrics track the return on investment for AI model selection and deployment. Agent productivity multipliers measure the efficiency gains from agent-augmented workflows. AI FinOps efficiency metrics show cost optimisation across inference, training, and infrastructure spend. These AI-specific metrics must be integrated alongside traditional EA value measures to give stakeholders a complete picture of architecture performance.
what_changes: New KPIs: model ROI, agent productivity multiplier, AI FinOps efficiency, agent error rates
Performance reporting must include AI-specific dashboards alongside traditional EA metrics
Value measurement must distinguish phase-one productivity gains from phase-two revenue contribution
what_survives: EA cannot demonstrate its value, leading to budget cuts and loss of management support
Metrics are either not defined or too technical to resonate with business stakeholders
Performance data is collected but never acted upon

traditional.summary: Define, standardize and continuously improve the processes that drive EA delivery.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Designing, documenting and continuously improving the core EA processes, ensuring a consistent, repeatable and efficient way of working across all architecture activities.

Tasks

  1. Identify and define core EA processes (e.g. architecture development, review, governance, repository management).
  2. Document processes including activities, roles, inputs, outputs and decision points.
  3. Align EA processes with existing organizational frameworks (e.g. project methodology, ITIL, SAFe).
  4. Conduct periodic process maturity assessments and identify improvement areas.
  5. Implement process improvements and track their effectiveness.

Inputs

  • EA capability and process maturity assessment results
  • Existing organizational process frameworks
  • Lessons learned and feedback from EA practitioners
  • Industry best practices and reference frameworks

Outputs

  • Documented EA process descriptions and workflows
  • EA process maturity assessment results
  • Process improvement backlog and action plans
  • Updated EA methodology and ways of working

ai_impact.summary: EA processes must accommodate <mark>AI's pace of change: continuous compliance, automated discovery, event-driven governance</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: EA processes designed for quarterly review cycles and annual planning horizons cannot keep pace with AI-driven change. Processes must evolve to support continuous compliance checking, automated architecture discovery from live environments, and event-driven governance that responds to changes in real-time. This means embedding agent-assisted steps into EA processes themselves — using AI agents to perform discovery, draft analyses, and flag compliance issues, while human architects focus on strategic decisions and exception handling.
what_changes: EA processes must support continuous compliance checking rather than periodic reviews
Automated architecture discovery from live environments replaces manual documentation
Agent-assisted EA processes where AI performs discovery and drafting while humans direct strategy
what_survives: EA work is done differently by every architect with no consistent methodology
Processes exist on paper but are not followed in practice
No mechanism to learn and improve and the same mistakes are repeated

traditional.summary: Capture and share architecture knowledge to reduce key-person dependency.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Establishing practices and platforms to systematically capture, organize, share and reuse architecture knowledge, decisions, patterns and lessons learned across the EA function and the wider organization.

Tasks

  1. Define the knowledge scope — what needs to be captured (e.g. architecture decisions, patterns, reference architectures, lessons learned).
  2. Establish a knowledge platform or wiki with a clear structure and taxonomy.
  3. Integrate knowledge management into daily EA work — capture decisions and rationale as part of the architecture process.
  4. Facilitate regular knowledge sharing sessions (e.g. architecture community of practice, brown bags).
  5. Maintain and curate knowledge base — archive outdated content, ensure findability and relevance.

Inputs

  • Architecture decisions and decision logs
  • Reference architectures, patterns and templates
  • Lessons learned from projects and reviews
  • EA process documentation and guidelines

Outputs

  • Structured EA knowledge base and wiki
  • Architecture decision records (ADRs)
  • Reference architecture and pattern library
  • Knowledge sharing session materials and recordings

ai_impact.summary: Must support <mark>machine-readable knowledge for AI agent consumption; knowledge graphs as shared enterprise context</mark>; Enterprise as Code.
ai_impact.body_html: Knowledge management must evolve from human-readable documentation to dual-format content that serves both human architects and AI agents. Knowledge graphs become the shared enterprise context that enables agents to make architecture-aware decisions. The Enterprise as Code paradigm codifies implicit operating models — workflows, decision rules, governance logic — as executable code readable by both humans and machines. Architecture decision records, patterns, and reference architectures must be structured in machine-readable formats that agents can query and apply.
what_changes: Knowledge must be machine-readable for AI agent consumption, not just human-readable documentation
Knowledge graphs become the shared enterprise context enabling agent decision-making
Enterprise as Code codifies implicit operating models as executable, machine-readable code
what_survives: Critical knowledge exists only in people's heads and is lost when they leave
Documentation exists but is scattered, outdated or unfindable
No culture of knowledge sharing as architects work in silos

traditional.summary: Foster stakeholder support and involvement and ensure effective communication and collaboration.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Stakeholder Management involves identifying, analysing, and engaging stakeholders to ensure their needs and concerns are addressed throughout the architecture development process. It aims to build and maintain stakeholder support and alignment.

Tasks

  1. Identify stakeholders and create a stakeholder map. Assign priorities and identify preferred communication channels, styles and frequencies.
  2. Analyse stakeholder interests and influence.
  3. Develop stakeholder engagement strategies.
  4. Plan the stakeholder communication and engagement.
  5. Regularly collect stakeholder feedback on EA services and adjust engagement approach accordingly.
  6. Manage escalations and resolve conflicting stakeholder interests in alignment with architecture priorities.

Inputs

  • Business strategy and goals
  • Stakeholder requirements and concerns
  • Existing stakeholder documentation

Outputs

  • Stakeholder map
  • Stakeholder analysis reports
  • Communication and engagement plans

ai_impact.summary: New stakeholders: <mark>AI agent owners, AI ethics boards, data sovereignty officers</mark>. Agents themselves have system requirements that function like stakeholder concerns.
ai_impact.body_html: Stakeholder management must expand to include entirely new stakeholder groups that emerge with AI deployment. AI agent owners are accountable for agent behaviour and performance. AI ethics boards govern responsible AI deployment. Data sovereignty officers ensure jurisdictional compliance. Beyond new human stakeholders, agents themselves generate system requirements — for context access, API availability, and processing capacity — that function like stakeholder concerns and must be managed through similar engagement practices.
what_changes: New stakeholder groups: AI agent owners, AI ethics boards, data sovereignty officers
Agents themselves generate requirements that function like stakeholder concerns
Stakeholder engagement must address AI workforce anxiety and transformation resistance
what_survives: Lack of trust, support and stakeholder engagement for transformation initiatives
Ambiguity around stakeholder roles and responsibilities causing confusion
Even getting a list of relevant stakeholders requires time and effort

traditional.summary: Establish the guardrails that ensure consistency and alignment across all architecture decisions.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Defining, maintaining and communicating the set of architecture principles, standards, policies and guidelines that govern how architecture is developed, implemented and managed across the organization.

Tasks

  1. Define architecture principles that reflect business values and strategic direction.
  2. Establish technology standards and policies (e.g. approved platforms, security requirements, integration patterns).
  3. Develop practical guidelines that translate principles and standards into actionable guidance for architects and project teams.
  4. Implement a review and approval process for new or updated standards.
  5. Communicate and socialize principles, standards and guidelines across the organization.
  6. Periodically review and update to ensure continued relevance.

Inputs

  • Business and IT strategy
  • Regulatory and compliance requirements
  • Industry standards and best practices
  • Architecture decision logs and lessons learned

Outputs

  • Architecture principles catalogue
  • Technology standards and policies documentation
  • Architecture guidelines and design patterns
  • Standards review and update log

ai_impact.summary: Must include <mark>AI-specific standards: model governance, agent communication, data sovereignty, AI ethics</mark>. Policy-as-code for automated enforcement.
ai_impact.body_html: The standards and policies framework must expand significantly to cover AI-specific governance requirements. Model governance standards define how AI models are selected, evaluated, deployed, and retired. Agent communication standards establish protocols and patterns for agent-to-agent interaction. Data sovereignty policies constrain how data flows to AI processing services across jurisdictions. AI ethics guidelines ensure responsible agent behaviour aligned with organisational values. Critically, these standards must be expressible as policy-as-code for automated enforcement in CI/CD pipelines.
what_changes: AI-specific standards: model governance, agent communication protocols, data sovereignty, AI ethics
Policy-as-code enables automated enforcement of architecture standards in CI/CD pipelines
Standards must evolve rapidly to keep pace with AI technology changes
what_survives: No defined standards so every project makes its own technology choices, creating fragmentation
Standards exist but are not communicated or enforced
Standards are too rigid or outdated, leading architects to bypass them

traditional.summary: Build architecture skills and awareness across the organization to drive EA adoption.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Planning, developing and delivering training programs that build architecture knowledge, skills and awareness among EA team members, IT staff and business stakeholders.

Tasks

  1. Assess training needs based on role requirements, skill gaps and EA maturity targets.
  2. Develop a training catalogue covering EA frameworks, tools, methods and soft skills.
  3. Deliver training through appropriate formats (e.g. workshops, e-learning, certifications, lunch and learns).
  4. Onboard new architects with a structured EA onboarding program.
  5. Evaluate training effectiveness and continuously update the training offering.

Inputs

  • EA role descriptions and competency matrix
  • Skills gap analysis results
  • EA tools and methodology documentation
  • Stakeholder feedback and training requests

Outputs

  • EA training catalogue and learning paths
  • Training materials and session recordings
  • Training completion and effectiveness reports
  • Onboarding program for new EA team members

ai_impact.summary: Must cover <mark>AI literacy, model evaluation, agent design, prompt engineering, AI ethics</mark>. Training must reach beyond EA team to business stakeholders.
ai_impact.body_html: Architecture training must expand dramatically in both scope and audience. All EA team members need AI literacy covering model capabilities, limitations, and governance requirements. Specialist tracks must cover model evaluation techniques, agent design patterns, prompt engineering, and AI ethics frameworks. Critically, training must reach beyond the EA team to business stakeholders who will work alongside agents — they need to understand agent capabilities, appropriate use cases, and escalation procedures. The training programme itself can leverage AI-assisted learning for personalised, continuous skill development.
what_changes: AI literacy becomes mandatory training for all EA roles: model capabilities, limitations, and governance
New specialist tracks: model evaluation, agent design, prompt engineering, AI ethics
Training audience expands beyond EA to business stakeholders who work alongside agents
what_survives: No structured training so new architects learn by trial and error
Training focuses only on tools and frameworks, neglecting communication and stakeholder skills
Knowledge stays with senior architects with no mechanism to transfer it broadly

traditional.summary: Drive awareness, understanding and adoption of Enterprise Architecture across the organization.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Planning and executing communication and change management activities to ensure that architecture initiatives, decisions and standards are understood, accepted and adopted by all relevant stakeholders.

Tasks

  1. Develop an EA communication strategy and plan covering target audiences, channels, frequency and key messages.
  2. Create and distribute regular EA communications (e.g. newsletters, architecture updates, decision summaries).
  3. Identify change impacts of architecture decisions and develop change management approaches.
  4. Engage stakeholders through targeted formats (e.g. town halls, roadshows, architecture days).
  5. Monitor adoption and gather feedback to continuously improve communication and change efforts.

Inputs

  • Architecture decisions and roadmaps
  • Stakeholder map and communication preferences
  • Change impact assessments
  • Stakeholder feedback and adoption metrics

Outputs

  • EA communication strategy and plan
  • Communication materials (newsletters, updates, presentations)
  • Change impact assessments and mitigation plans
  • Adoption and feedback reports

ai_impact.summary: Must address <mark>AI transformation resistance, agent workforce integration, AI value communication</mark>. The 10/20/70 rule applies.
ai_impact.body_html: Communication and change management become critical success factors for AI adoption. The 10/20/70 rule — where 10% of AI transformation effort is algorithms, 20% is technology, and 70% is people and process change — underscores that technical deployment is the easy part. Communication must proactively address workforce anxiety about agent deployment, clearly articulate the human-agent collaboration model, and demonstrate AI value through concrete examples. Change management must support the transition to new ways of working where agents handle routine tasks and humans focus on judgment, creativity, and exception handling.
what_changes: Must proactively address AI transformation resistance and workforce anxiety about agent deployment
Communication must articulate the human-agent collaboration model and demonstrate concrete AI value
Change management follows the 10/20/70 rule: 10% algorithms, 20% technology, 70% people and process
what_survives: EA works in an ivory tower with great architecture that nobody knows about or follows
Communication is ad-hoc and reactive and stakeholders only hear from EA when there is a problem
Architecture changes are pushed without change management, leading to resistance

traditional.summary: Ensure architecture decisions and implementations adhere to defined standards and principles.
traditional.body_html:
Definition

Assessing and evaluating whether projects, solutions and implementations comply with established architecture principles, standards, policies and guidelines, and managing deviations through a structured waiver process.

Tasks

  1. Define compliance evaluation criteria based on architecture principles, standards and policies.
  2. Conduct architecture compliance reviews at key project milestones (e.g. design, build, go-live).
  3. Document compliance findings, deviations and associated risks.
  4. Manage a structured waiver process for justified deviations — including conditions and expiry.
  5. Report compliance status to the Architecture Review Board and relevant stakeholders.

Inputs

  • Architecture principles, standards and policies
  • Project and solution architecture documentation
  • Waiver requests and deviation justifications
  • Previous compliance review findings

Outputs

  • Compliance assessment reports
  • Deviation and waiver register
  • Risk assessments for non-compliant decisions
  • Compliance status dashboards and trend reports

ai_impact.summary: Must shift from <mark>periodic manual review to continuous automated compliance</mark>. AI-specific compliance criteria: model governance, agent behaviour, bias monitoring, sovereignty.
ai_impact.body_html: Architecture compliance evaluation must fundamentally transform from periodic manual checkpoint reviews to continuous automated compliance monitoring. Policy-as-code enables compliance rules to be checked automatically in CI/CD pipelines at deployment time, catching violations before they reach production. AI-specific compliance criteria must address model governance (approved models, version control), agent behaviour (permission boundaries, escalation rules), bias monitoring (fairness metrics, demographic parity), and sovereignty compliance (data residency, cross-border inference). The waiver process must support the rapid pace of AI deployment without becoming a bottleneck.
what_changes: Shift from periodic manual reviews to continuous automated compliance via policy-as-code
AI-specific compliance criteria: model governance, agent behaviour, bias monitoring, sovereignty
Compliance must operate at deployment speed through CI/CD pipeline integration
what_survives: No formal compliance checks so deviations are only discovered in production
Compliance is perceived as a bottleneck and projects bypass architecture reviews
Waivers are granted informally without tracking, leading to unmanaged technical debt

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: <mark>Onboarding, monitoring, versioning, retraining, and retiring AI agents</mark> as enterprise workforce participants.
ai_impact.body_html: Agent Lifecycle Management governs the complete lifecycle of AI agents from initial onboarding through active deployment to eventual retirement. It includes agent registry maintenance, performance monitoring, version management, retraining triggers, and decommissioning procedures. This capability treats agents as workforce participants with defined onboarding processes, performance expectations, escalation procedures, and retirement criteria — analogous to human workforce management but adapted for autonomous software agents.
what_changes: Agent registry tracks all deployed agents with configuration, permissions, and performance history
Lifecycle stages: onboarding, active, under-review, retraining, deprecated, retired
Performance management includes task completion rates, error rates, escalation frequency, and cost efficiency

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: Managing the enterprise's <mark>portfolio of AI models: selection, benchmarking, cost tracking, vendor diversification, and deprecation planning</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: Model Portfolio Management applies portfolio management discipline to the enterprise's collection of AI models. It governs model selection through structured evaluation criteria, tracks model performance through continuous benchmarking, manages costs through inference monitoring and right-sizing, ensures vendor diversification to avoid lock-in, and plans for deprecation as models become obsolete. This capability directly reduces AI cost and mitigates vendor concentration risk.
what_changes: Structured model selection process with evaluation criteria: accuracy, cost, latency, sovereignty, vendor risk
Continuous benchmarking compares deployed models against newer alternatives
Vendor diversification strategy prevents concentration risk on a single AI provider

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.
