ai_impact.summary: <mark>Financial governance for AI operations</mark>: inference cost tracking, fine-tuning budgets, vector storage costs, infrastructure cost allocation.
ai_impact.body_html: AI FinOps establishes financial governance for AI operations across the enterprise. It tracks inference costs by model, workload, and business unit; manages fine-tuning budgets and training compute spend; monitors vector storage and knowledge graph costs; allocates infrastructure costs to AI workloads; and implements budget thresholds with escalation triggers. Only 10% of organisations have mature AI FinOps practices, making this a critical gap for most enterprises scaling AI deployment.
what_changes: Inference cost tracking by model, workload, and business unit with budget thresholds
Fine-tuning and training compute budget management with ROI tracking
Cost allocation framework that attributes AI infrastructure costs to consuming business units

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: <mark>Automated, continuous compliance checking integrated into CI/CD pipelines</mark>. Policy-as-code with automated enforcement.
ai_impact.body_html: Continuous Architecture Compliance moves compliance from periodic human review to automated, continuous checking embedded in CI/CD pipelines. Architecture standards and policies are codified as executable rules (policy-as-code) that are evaluated automatically at deployment time. This enables governance at the speed of AI deployment — catching violations before they reach production without creating bottlenecks. While traditional compliance evaluation focuses on manual review, this capability is about automated enforcement infrastructure.
what_changes: Policy-as-code translates architecture standards into executable compliance rules
CI/CD pipeline integration checks compliance at deployment time automatically
Real-time compliance dashboards replace periodic manual compliance reports

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: Treating <mark>data residency, model provenance, and jurisdictional constraints as first-order architecture design inputs</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: Sovereignty-by-Design treats data residency, model provenance, and jurisdictional constraints as first-order architecture design inputs rather than afterthought compliance checks. It maps AI workloads to sovereignty requirements, constrains infrastructure decisions based on jurisdictional rules, and ensures model training data provenance meets regulatory standards. This is critical for EU and multi-jurisdictional organisations where AI regulation creates binding constraints on architecture decisions.
what_changes: Sovereignty requirements drive infrastructure selection, not the other way around
AI workload mapping to jurisdictional constraints becomes a standard architecture activity
Model provenance tracking ensures training data complies with data residency regulations

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: <mark>Codifying implicit operating models as executable code</mark> readable by both humans and AI agents. Prerequisite for effective agent deployment at scale.
ai_impact.body_html: Enterprise as Code codifies the implicit operating models that exist in organisations — workflows, decision rules, governance logic, escalation procedures — as executable code that is readable and actionable by both humans and AI agents. This is a prerequisite for effective agent deployment at scale: agents cannot follow unwritten rules or implicit knowledge. While high effort and low maturity today, this capability represents a compelling strategic direction that enables agents to operate within the full context of the enterprise.
what_changes: Implicit workflows, decision rules, and governance logic are codified as executable specifications
Dual-format content serves both human understanding and machine execution
Agents can operate within the full context of enterprise rules without human interpretation

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: <mark>Protocol abstraction layer for agent-to-agent communication</mark>, independent of specific protocol (MCP, A2A, ACP).
ai_impact.body_html: Agent Communication Architecture designs the inter-agent communication layer as a protocol-independent abstraction. With competing standards (MCP, A2A, ACP) and rapid protocol evolution, this capability ensures the enterprise can adopt and switch between agent communication protocols without re-architecting agent interactions. This is the defining architectural bet for 2026–2028: organisations that lock into a single protocol risk costly re-architecture as standards consolidate.
what_changes: Protocol abstraction layer enables agent communication independent of specific standard (MCP, A2A, ACP)
Agent interaction patterns are defined at the semantic level, not the protocol level
Multi-protocol support enables gradual migration as agent communication standards mature

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.

ai_impact.summary: <mark>Structured assessment of compute, network, storage, and integration capacity</mark> against planned AI workloads.
ai_impact.body_html: AI Infrastructure Readiness Assessment provides a structured framework for evaluating whether the enterprise's compute, network, storage, and integration infrastructure can support planned AI workloads. With only 22% of organisations reporting infrastructure readiness for AI at scale, this is a binding constraint for AI deployment. The assessment includes GPU compute capacity, high-bandwidth networking for model training, vector storage scaling, sovereignty constraint mapping, and integration capacity for agent communication patterns.
what_changes: Structured readiness assessment framework covering GPU compute, networking, storage, and integration
Sovereignty constraint mapping identifies where infrastructure gaps create regulatory risk
Capacity planning models for AI workloads with different scaling characteristics than traditional applications

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: <mark>Distributed tracing across agent interactions, blast-radius controls, cascade failure detection, circuit breakers</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: Agent Risk and Observability addresses the unique risk profile of autonomous, non-deterministic AI agents operating at enterprise scale. It implements distributed tracing across agent interactions to enable end-to-end visibility, blast-radius controls to limit the impact of agent failures, cascade failure detection to identify chain reactions across multi-agent systems, and circuit breakers to automatically halt agents exhibiting anomalous behaviour. This capability is required before deploying a second agent, as multi-agent interactions create emergent risks that single-agent deployments do not.
what_changes: Distributed tracing enables end-to-end visibility across multi-agent interaction chains
Blast-radius controls and circuit breakers limit the impact of agent failures
Cascade failure detection identifies chain reactions across interconnected agent systems

traditional.summary: No traditional EA equivalent exists.
traditional.body_html: This capability has no equivalent in traditional EA practice. It addresses requirements that arise only when AI agents become part of the enterprise workforce.

ai_impact.summary: Planning the <mark>platform transition from productivity (phase one) to revenue (phase two) AI</mark>.
ai_impact.body_html: AI Value Progression Management addresses the strategic transition from phase-one AI (productivity gains from cost reduction and efficiency) to phase-two AI (revenue generation from AI-augmented products and services). It aligns CEO and CIO expectations on AI value timelines, maps the platform requirements for each phase, and defines differentiated KPIs that track progress through the value progression. This capability addresses the C-suite expectation misalignment where leadership expects revenue impact while the organisation is still building productivity foundations.
what_changes: Phase-one to phase-two transition planning aligns expectations on AI value timelines
Differentiated KPIs track productivity gains (phase one) separately from revenue generation (phase two)
Platform requirement mapping ensures infrastructure investments support the value progression
